title: Foundations Vocabulary
description: Essential terminology for body organization, chemistry, cells, and membranes

questions.q: Which term describes the study of body structures and their relationships?
questions.explain: Anatomy is the study of body structures. The word comes from Greek 'anatome' meaning 'dissection' — literally cutting apart to see what's inside.

optionExplains.text: Physiology studies how structures FUNCTION, not what they ARE. Different question entirely.
Anatomy examines structure — the architecture of the body. Where things are, how they connect, what they look like. It's the blueprint before the building starts working.
Histology is the study of tissues specifically — a subset of anatomy that zooms in to the microscopic level.
Pathology studies disease processes — what goes wrong, not what's there when things are right.

questions.q: The term 'homeostasis' refers to:
options: The maintenance of stable internal conditions
questions.explain: Homeostasis means 'staying the same' — the body's ability to maintain stable internal conditions despite external changes. Temperature, pH, glucose levels — all held within narrow ranges.

optionExplains.text: That's histology. Homeostasis is about stability, not tissue structure.
Homeostasis is dynamic equilibrium — the body constantly adjusting to keep internal conditions stable. Your core temperature stays around 37°C whether you're in a sauna or a snowstorm.
That describes catabolism — breaking molecules down. Homeostasis is about balance, not breakdown.
Cell formation is proliferation or mitosis. Homeostasis includes regulating cell numbers, but it's much broader than that.

questions.q: Which body cavity contains the heart and lungs?
questions.explain: The thoracic cavity is the chest cavity — bounded by the ribs, protected by the sternum. It houses the heart, lungs, and great vessels.

optionExplains.text: The abdominal cavity holds digestive organs — stomach, intestines, liver. Below the diaphragm, not above.
The pelvic cavity contains reproductive organs and bladder. It's the bowl at the bottom of your trunk.
The thoracic cavity — your ribcage fortress. The heart sits medially in the mediastinum, lungs flanking it on either side. The diaphragm forms the floor.
The cranial cavity houses the brain. Different neighborhood entirely — upstairs, not chest-level.

questions.q: The anatomical term 'proximal' means:
options: Closer to the trunk or point of attachment
Further from the trunk or point of attachment
questions.explain: Proximal means closer to the origin or attachment point. Your elbow is proximal to your wrist — closer to where the arm connects to the body.

optionExplains.text: That's posterior or dorsal. Proximal is about distance from the trunk, not front-back orientation.
That's anterior or ventral. Again, wrong axis — proximal measures distance along a limb.
Proximal means 'nearer to the root.' The shoulder is proximal to the elbow, the elbow proximal to the wrist, the wrist proximal to the fingers.
That's distal — the opposite of proximal. Distal means further from the trunk.

questions.q: Which plane divides the body into anterior and posterior portions?
questions.explain: The frontal or coronal plane runs vertically from side to side, dividing the body into front (anterior) and back (posterior) portions.

optionExplains.text: Sagittal planes divide left from right, not front from back.
The frontal plane — also called coronal because it aligns with the coronal suture of the skull — slices you into front and back halves. Like a pane of glass from ear to ear.
Transverse planes are horizontal — they divide superior from inferior, creating cross-sections.
Midsagittal is a specific sagittal plane down the exact middle — still divides left from right, not front from back.

questions.q: The smallest living unit of the body is the:
questions.explain: The cell is the fundamental unit of life. Everything living is made of cells — they're the smallest structures that can perform all life functions independently.

optionExplains.text: Organs are made OF cells — they're collections of tissues working together. Too big.
Tissues are groups of similar cells. Still too large — you need to zoom in further.
The cell is life's basic unit. It can metabolize, reproduce, respond to stimuli, maintain homeostasis — all the criteria for life, wrapped in one membrane-bound package.
Organ systems are the largest organizational level — groups of organs cooperating. Way too big.

questions.q: Which term describes a solution with a pH below 7?
questions.explain: Acidic solutions have pH values below 7. They contain excess hydrogen ions (H+). The lower the pH, the more acidic — stomach acid is around pH 2.

optionExplains.text: Basic solutions have pH ABOVE 7. They're also called alkaline.
Neutral is exactly pH 7 — pure water. Neither acidic nor basic.
Acidic means pH below 7, indicating excess H+ ions. Gastric juice, lemon juice, vinegar — all acidic. The lower the number, the stronger the acid.
Alkaline is another word for basic — pH above 7. Opposite of acidic.

questions.q: The organelle responsible for ATP production is the:
questions.explain: Mitochondria are the powerhouses of the cell. They perform cellular respiration, converting glucose and oxygen into ATP — the energy currency cells actually spend.

optionExplains.text: Ribosomes make proteins by reading mRNA instructions. Important, but not energy production.
The Golgi apparatus packages and ships proteins. It's the post office, not the power plant.
Mitochondria — double-membraned, cristae-folded, ATP-generating machines. They run the citric acid cycle and electron transport chain to harvest energy from food.
Lysosomes digest cellular waste. They're recycling centers, not energy factories.

questions.q: The plasma membrane is described as 'selectively permeable' because it:
options: Allows all substances to pass through
Allows some substances to pass while blocking others
questions.explain: Selectively permeable means the membrane chooses what crosses. Small nonpolar molecules slip through; charged ions need channels; large molecules need transporters.

optionExplains.text: If it allowed everything through, it wouldn't be selective — it would be freely permeable. The cell would have no control.
If it blocked everything, the cell would starve. Nutrients couldn't enter, wastes couldn't exit.

Selective permeability is the membrane's superpower. It creates controlled chaos — O2 and CO2 diffuse freely, but glucose needs GLUT transporters, sodium needs channels. The membrane is a bouncer with a very specific guest list.
The membrane functions constantly, not just during division. Permeability is a 24/7 operation.

questions.q: Which type of tissue covers body surfaces and lines cavities?
questions.explain: Epithelial tissue forms coverings and linings — skin surfaces, organ linings, gland secretory units. It's the body's interface layer.

optionExplains.text: Connective tissue supports and binds — bone, cartilage, blood, fat. It doesn't form surface coverings.
Muscle tissue contracts for movement. It doesn't line surfaces.
Nervous tissue transmits signals. It's communication infrastructure, not surface coverage.
Epithelial tissue lines and covers everything. Skin epidermis, mouth lining, stomach lining, kidney tubules, blood vessel interiors — all epithelium. It's the body's gift wrap.

questions.q: The term 'lateral' refers to a position:
questions.explain: Lateral means away from the midline — toward the side. Your ears are lateral to your nose. Your shoulders are lateral to your sternum.

optionExplains.text: That's medial — toward the midline. The opposite of lateral.
Lateral means 'to the side.' The lungs are lateral to the heart. The eyes are lateral to the nose. Lateral moves you away from the body's center line.
That's superior — above. Different directional axis entirely.
That's inferior — below. Again, wrong axis.

questions.q: Negative feedback mechanisms:
options: Amplify changes from the set point
Oppose changes to return to the set point
questions.explain: Negative feedback opposes change. When something deviates from the set point, negative feedback pushes it back. It's the body's thermostat principle.

optionExplains.text: That's positive feedback — amplification. Negative feedback does the opposite.

Negative feedback maintains homeostasis by opposing deviations. Blood glucose rises → insulin secretion → glucose drops back down. Temperature rises → sweating → temperature drops. Always pushing back toward the set point.
Negative feedback operates constantly in health. It's how you stay alive, not a disease response.
Temperature regulation uses negative feedback to STABILIZE temperature, not increase it continuously.

questions.q: The serous membrane that lines the abdominal cavity wall is called the:
questions.explain: Parietal peritoneum lines the abdominal WALL. Parietal means 'wall' — it's the membrane against the body cavity's outer boundary.

optionExplains.text: Visceral peritoneum covers the ORGANS, not the wall. Visceral means 'organ.'
Parietal peritoneum lines the wall — parietal always means 'wall layer.' It's the outer sheet of the double membrane, pressed against the abdominal wall.
Pleura is the serous membrane of the lungs and thoracic cavity, not the abdomen.
Pericardium is the serous membrane around the heart specifically.

questions.q: Which macromolecule stores genetic information?
questions.explain: Nucleic acids — DNA and RNA — store and transmit genetic information. DNA holds the permanent instructions; RNA copies and delivers them.

optionExplains.text: Carbohydrates store energy and provide structure. No information storage — they're fuel, not files.
Lipids store energy and build membranes. Still no genetic information capacity.
Proteins do the work encoded by genes, but they don't store the genetic information itself.
Nucleic acids — DNA's double helix holds the master blueprint, RNA reads and executes it. Four bases encode the entire instruction manual for life.

questions.q: The movement of water across a selectively permeable membrane from high to low water concentration is called:
questions.explain: Osmosis is specifically water diffusion across a membrane. Water moves from where it's more concentrated (dilute solution) to where it's less concentrated (concentrated solution).

optionExplains.text: Diffusion is the general term for any substance moving down its concentration gradient. Osmosis is specifically water.
Active transport requires ATP and moves substances AGAINST their gradient. Osmosis is passive.
Osmosis — water's special journey across membranes. It follows the gradient passively, always trying to dilute the more concentrated side.
Filtration is pressure-driven movement through a membrane, like in the kidneys. Different mechanism.

questions.q: The region of the cell containing genetic material and surrounded by a double membrane is the:
questions.explain: The nucleus houses DNA within its double-membrane envelope. Nuclear pores control traffic between the nucleus and cytoplasm.

optionExplains.text: Cytoplasm is the gel-like material outside the nucleus. It contains organelles but not the main genetic storage.
The nucleus — command center enclosed by a double membrane studded with pores. DNA stays safe inside, directing cellular operations through messenger RNA.
Ribosomes are protein factories. They're not membrane-bound and don't store DNA.
The ER processes proteins and lipids. It's connected to the nuclear envelope but isn't the genetic storage site.

questions.q: Which directional term means 'toward the head'?
questions.explain: Superior or cranial means toward the head end. The heart is superior to the intestines. The brain is the most superior structure.

optionExplains.text: Caudal means toward the tail — the opposite direction. Used more in animal anatomy.
Inferior means toward the feet — below. Opposite of superior.
Superior or cranial — headward. The nose is superior to the chin. The lungs are superior to the liver. Always pointing up toward the skull.
Posterior means toward the back, not toward the head. Different axis.

questions.q: A covalent bond forms when atoms:
questions.explain: Covalent bonds form when atoms share electrons. Each atom contributes to a shared pair, creating a strong, stable bond.

optionExplains.text: Complete electron transfer creates ionic bonds, not covalent. Different bonding mechanism.
Covalent bonding is electron sharing. Two atoms each contribute electrons to a shared orbital, binding them together. Carbon-carbon bonds, oxygen-hydrogen bonds — all covalent.
That describes ionic bonds — oppositely charged ions attracting after electron transfer.
Atoms don't lose protons in chemical bonding. Protons define the element itself.

questions.q: The transverse plane divides the body into:
questions.explain: The transverse plane is horizontal — it creates cross-sections, dividing the body into upper (superior) and lower (inferior) portions.

optionExplains.text: That's what sagittal planes do — divide left from right.
That's the frontal/coronal plane — dividing front from back.
Transverse planes run horizontally, like slicing a loaf of bread. CT scans show transverse sections — each slice divides what's above from what's below.
Proximal and distal describe positions along limbs, not body plane divisions.

questions.q: The organelle that modifies, packages, and ships proteins is the:
questions.explain: The Golgi apparatus is the cell's shipping department. Proteins arrive from the ER, get modified and packaged into vesicles, then shipped to their destinations.

optionExplains.text: Ribosomes MAKE proteins but don't modify or ship them. That's the Golgi's job.
The Golgi apparatus — stacked cisternae that receive, modify, and dispatch proteins. Add sugar groups, trim sequences, sort by destination, package into vesicles. It's FedEx for molecules.
Mitochondria make ATP. They're power plants, not post offices.
The nucleus stores DNA and coordinates gene expression. Protein shipping happens in the cytoplasm.

questions.q: Which term describes the front surface of the body?
questions.explain: Anterior or ventral refers to the front of the body. Your belly button is on your anterior surface. Your face is anterior to your brain.

optionExplains.text: Posterior means back — the opposite of anterior.
Dorsal also means back (think dorsal fin on a fish). Same as posterior in humans.
Anterior or ventral — the front. Your chest is anterior. Your nose is anterior. In anatomical position, anterior faces forward.
Lateral means to the side, not front or back.

questions.q: Enzymes are which type of macromolecule?
questions.explain: Enzymes are proteins that catalyze biochemical reactions. Their 3D shape creates active sites where substrates bind and reactions accelerate.

optionExplains.text: Carbohydrates are energy sources and structural molecules, not catalysts.
Lipids store energy and build membranes. They don't catalyze reactions.
Enzymes are proteins — specifically, catalytic proteins. Their amino acid sequence folds into shapes that grab specific substrates and speed reactions millions-fold.
Nucleic acids store genetic information. Some RNA molecules have catalytic activity (ribozymes), but most enzymes are proteins.

questions.q: The epigastric region is located:
questions.explain: The epigastric region is the upper central abdomen — 'epi' means above, 'gastric' refers to stomach. It's above the umbilicus, below the sternum.

optionExplains.text: That would be the hypogastric region — 'hypo' means below.
Epigastric — 'epi' (above) + 'gastric' (stomach). This central upper abdominal region sits above the umbilicus, housing parts of the stomach, liver, and pancreas.
The lateral regions are the lumbar (flanks) and iliac regions. Epigastric is central.
Abdominal regions are on the anterior surface. There's no posterior epigastric region.

questions.q: Active transport differs from passive transport because it:
options: Moves substances down their concentration gradient
questions.explain: Active transport requires ATP because it moves substances AGAINST their concentration gradient — from low to high concentration. It's swimming upstream.

optionExplains.text: That's passive transport — movement down the gradient requires no energy input.
Active transport burns ATP to move molecules against the gradient. The sodium-potassium pump uses ATP to push 3 Na+ out and 2 K+ in — against both ions' natural tendencies.
Water movement (osmosis) is passive. Active transport moves ions, glucose, amino acids — many substances.
Active transport absolutely requires membrane proteins — the pumps and carriers that use ATP.

questions.q: The hypodermis (subcutaneous layer) is located:
questions.explain: The hypodermis lies below the dermis — 'hypo' means under. It's the fatty layer connecting skin to underlying structures, providing insulation and cushioning.

optionExplains.text: Nothing is above the epidermis — it's the outermost layer.
The epidermis and dermis are directly connected. No layer between them.
Hypodermis — below the dermis. This subcutaneous fat layer anchors skin to deeper tissues, stores energy, and insulates against temperature extremes.
The hypodermis is part of the integumentary system, not inside body cavities.
